segments.s541-g81: Literal imperative clause: the verb فرست (an older/poetic imperative 'send') comes at the end. همه کودکان را is the definite direct object (همه 'all' + کودکان 'children' + رَا object marker), and به میدان marks the destination ('to the field').
segments.s541-g82: Purpose/infinitive clause introduced by به + infinitive: به بازیدن here marks the activity the children should undertake ('to play/engage in ...'); گوی و چوگان is the coordinated object of that activity ('ball and polo/mallet'), with a repeated imperative فرست omitted subject (same command).
segments.s541-g83: چو introduces a comparison/temporal clause ('when/as if'); دشت کودک بود is a noun clause 'there was a plain of children' with بود as past tense copula, and خوب‌چهر is a predicate/adjectival phrase 'fair‑faced' describing the children or scene.
segments.s541-g84: بپیچد (ب‑ + پیچد) is a poetic/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'would turn/be wrapped' or 'might be moved'; ز marks the cause or origin ('out of/because of'), فرزند جانم is a possessive phrase ('my dear child' — جانم = 'my life/beloved'), and به مهر means 'toward love/with affection.' The clause expresses the speaker's heart turning to affection for the child.
segments.s541-g85: بدان راستی is a prepositional/pointing phrase ('to/with that truth' — here بدان = به + آن in poetic form). دل is the subject 'the heart' and گواهی دهد ('give testimony') uses دهد in an optative/jussive sense ('let it give' / 'may it give'), so the whole clause requests that the heart attest to that truth.
segments.s541-g86: مرا (here split as م + را) is the accusative 'me' (را marks the object); با پسر آشنایی دهد is a causative/benefactive construction 'make (me) acquainted with the son' where آشنایی (acquaintance/familiarity) is the thing given and دهد again is optative 'let it give' / 'may it grant.'
segments.s541-g49: بیاورد is a 3rd‑person past narrative verb (brought/produced) introducing the following noun phrase as its object. آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying حقه, which here names a physical pouch/device (not an abstract 'trick'). گنجور is an adjectival/epithet meaning 'treasure‑laden' attached to حقه, and اوی is the possessive clitic 'his/its' referring back to the agent.
segments.s541-g50: سپرد is a past tense verb 'entrusted/handed over' (3rd‑person). آنک is the relative/ demonstrative 'that which', linking what was handed over to the following clause. بستد is a past verb meaning 'fastened/closed/tied'; ز here means 'by/from' in the instrumental/causal sense, and دستور is 'order/command'. اوی is the possessive 'his', so the clause says it had been fastened by his command.
segments.s541-g51: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him' (indirect object). گفت here functions as the past verb 'said' in the narrative (the given sense list is nominal but context yields a speech verb). شاه is 'the king' (subject), اندرین = اندر + این 'in/inside this' (poetic word order), حقه is the pouch/case, چی is the interrogative 'what', and ست is the copula 'is' concluding the question 'what is (it)'.
segments.s541-g52: نهاده is a past participle/verb 'placed/put'. بر + ین (برین) = 'on this' (بر = on; ین here is the variant of این 'this'). بند is a physical 'band/fetter' or fastening. بر مهر = 'upon the seal' (مهر = seal/stamp). کیست is the interrogative 'who is it?' — the whole clause asks who put this band on the seal.
segments.s541-g53: بدو 'to him' again marks the indirect object. گفت is the past 'said'. کان here is a demonstrative ('that') in classical usage (no exact matching sense provided in the list), خون = 'blood', گرم = 'warm' (literal or emphatic), and منست in the text functions as 'is mine' (classical fusion of 'من است'), though the provided senses for منست do not match this possessive reading.
segments.s541-g54: بریده is a passive/past participle 'cut off' or 'severed'. ز = 'from'. بن = 'root' or 'stock' (here lineage/lineage root). پاک = 'pure'. شرم = 'shame' (the clause states that being severed from a pure root is a shame), and again منست functions as a copulative predicate ('is mine/it is my shame') though supplied senses do not capture that precisely.
segments.s541-g55: سپردی is 2nd‑person past 'you entrusted/handed over'. مرا is the object 'to me' (here tokenized into م + را; the provided senses for م do not fit this clitic form). را is the definite object marker attached to the clitic. دختر اردوان = 'the daughter of Ardavan' (direct object).
segments.s541-g56: که ... تا introduces a subordinate clause giving purpose/condition: که (that/so that) + تا (until). بازخواهی here denotes 'demand/claim back' (the noun/idea of reclaiming or the act of demanding return), تن = 'body', and بی‌روان = 'lifeless' (literally 'without soul'). The phrase conveys 'until you call/demand her back, (you will find) a lifeless body.'
segments.s541-g1: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (not a simile). هنگامه زادن is a nominal phrase 'the hour/moment of birth' (هنگامه = 'moment/crisis'; زادن here names the act of giving birth). آمد is the past tense verb 'came/arrived'. فراز is an adverbial particle meaning 'up/on high' or 'arose' placed after آمد for poetic word order.
segments.s541-g2: ازان کار = 'from that affair' (ازان = 'from that'; کار = 'affair/deed'). بر باد is a prepositional/idiomatic phrase 'to the wind' (بر = 'on/to'; باد = 'wind'), and نگشاد is the negated past form of گشاد 'did not open/reveal' (ن‑ prefix + گشاد). راز is the affected noun 'secret'. The clause means the secret was not revealed of/through that affair.
segments.s541-g3: پسر زاد is a compact past/passive expression meaning 'a son was born/was begotten' (پسر = 'son'; زاد here marks birth/being born). پس = 'after/then' linking the next item. دختر اردوان = 'Ardavan's daughter' (دختر = 'daughter', اردوان a proper name). Word order is terse and typical of epic poetry.
segments.s541-g4: یکی begins an appositive description 'one' or 'a certain one' referring back to the daughter. خسروآیین is an adjectival epithet 'kingly in manner/royal‑bearing'. و = 'and'. روشن‌روان is a compound epithet 'bright‑souled' or 'fair of spirit/face'. Together they describe the person just mentioned.
segments.s541-g5: از ایوان خویش = 'from his iwan/porch' (از = 'from'; ایوان = 'iwan/porch'; خوی + ش here function together as the possessive 'his own'). انجمن دور کرد is a verbal phrase 'he put the assembly at a distance / drove the assembly away' (دور کردن = 'to remove/banish'; کرد is the past auxiliary 'did/caused'). The object انجمن precedes the adverb دور in normal Persian poetic order, with the verb آخر.
segments.s541-g6: ورا = 'to him' or 'for him' (dative). نام دستور = 'the name/title Dastur' (نام = 'name', دستور here functions as a title). شاپور is the proper name attached to the title. کرد completes the verb 'he made/gave' (i.e. 'he gave him the name'). The structure = 'He gave him the name Dastur Shapur.'
segments.s541-g7: نهانش = نهان + ش: 'him/it hidden' or 'his hiding' used idiomatically here as 'he kept him hidden' (ش is the possessive/ object clitic). همی داشت is the imperfective/past continuous 'kept/was keeping' (همی adds durative aspect; داشت functions as auxiliary 'kept'). تا هفت سال = a duration 'for seven years' (تا here = 'until/for' with a period expression).
segments.s541-g8: یکی = 'one' (here introducing the subject who follows or meaning 'then one'); شاه نو گشت = 'became a new king' (شاه = 'king', نو = 'new', گشت = past of گشت 'became'). با فر و یال is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with glory and mane' (فر = royal glory/splendour; یال = 'mane', used metaphorically for royal crest/majesty).
segments.s541-g73: ازان پس is an archaic adverbial phrase meaning 'thereupon/after that.' چنین گفت is a past tense verbal phrase ('spoke thus' / 'thus he said'); با + person here functions as the preposition 'to/with' introducing the addressee. کدخدای is the addressee ('the village headman'). Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal phrase — comment — verb — prepositional phrase marking the person addressed.
segments.s541-g74: که‌ای here is the sequence introducing the direct address: که (that/introducer) + ای (vocative 'O'), effectively marking quoted speech; the following noun phrase is a vocative. مرد روشن‌دل و پاک‌رای is a coordinated noun + two epithets: مرد (man) + روشن‌دل (lit. 'clear‑hearted,' an adjectival compound) و پاک‑رای ('pure‑minded' or 'of good repute'). The adjective compounds follow the noun, as is common in Persian.
segments.s541-g75: بسی is an adverb meaning 'much/very' (classical usage). رنج is the noun 'suffering' and برداشتی is a past tense (2nd person) form of برداشتن used here to mean 'you have taken/experienced' — 'you have endured.' زین سخن is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from this speech' (ز + این = 'from this' + سخن 'speech'), specifying cause or source.
segments.s541-g76: نمانم is an optative/jussive sense here ('may it not remain' / 'let it not be left'), introducing a wish or admonition; it governs the subordinate clause beginning with که. که introduces the content clause 'that your suffering become old/long‑standing.' رنج تو گردد کهن: رنج 'suffering', تو 'your' (2nd person), گردد (subjunctive/optative of گردیدن/گشتن 'become'), کهن 'old/long‑standing.' Together they mean 'may your suffering not become permanent.'
segments.s541-g77: کنون is an adverb 'now.' صد پسر گیر uses گیر as an imperative 'take/bring' (classical imperative form) with صد پسر as its object 'a hundred sons.' همسال اوی is a genitive/descriptive phrase: همسال 'of the same age' and اوی is the archaic possessive/pronominal form 'his/of him' — 'a hundred sons of his same age' (i.e., peers to that boy).
segments.s541-g78: به here introduces the set of comparative attributes and functions like 'in/with' (i.e., 'matching in'). بالا, دوش, بر, یال list physical attributes: بالا 'height/loftiness', دوش 'shoulder', بر (here) 'back' (classical usage), یال 'mane' or crest. اوی again is the possessive 'his,' so the whole phrase says they should match him in these respects. Conjunction و links the items in the series.
segments.s541-g79: همان جامه پوشیده reads as a verbless or participial clause meaning 'dressed in the same garment(s).' همان 'the same,' جامه 'garment,' پوشیده is a participle/adjectival past form meaning 'covered/dressed' (here functioning as 'wearing' or 'clothed in'). با او بهم: با او 'with him' and بهم 'together' (so 'together with him they should wear the same clothes').
segments.s541-g80: نباید is the impersonal modal 'should not / must not,' introducing a negative necessity; that + clause structure follows (که). چیزی بود بیش و کم literally 'that anything be more or less' — بود (to be) here used in a jussive/indicative sense governed by نباید; بیش و کم 'more and less' = 'excess or deficiency.' The whole clause means 'there should be nothing more or less.'
segments.s541-g65: چنو is an archaic/poetic form meaning “such” or “so”; نیست is the present/past-negation 'is not/does not exist' used impersonally here; فرزند یک شاه را is a noun phrase 'a child of a king' with را as the definite/object marker attached to the whole phrase. Word order is topical/poetic: the qualifier (چنو) and the negative predicate (نیست) precede the object phrase.
segments.s541-g66: نماند is the past/preterite sense 'was left / remained' (used impersonally: 'there remained'); مگر introduces the exception 'except'; بر فلک is a locative 'upon the firmament/sky'; ماه را is the exception phrase 'the moon' with را marking it as the focussed/definite item. The clause literally says 'there remained not, except on the sky the moon.'
segments.s541-g67: ورا is the dative 'to him' placed before the object; نام شاپور کردم is the verbal expression 'I made/gave the name Shapur' (کردم = 1st person past 'I did/made' so 'I named him Shapur'); ز مهر is an ablative/reasonal phrase 'out of affection'. The sequence 'ورا نام ... کردم ز مهر' = 'I named him Shapur out of love.'
segments.s541-g68: که introduces the following wish/clause; از بخت تو is a prepositional phrase 'because of your fortune' (از = 'because of/from'); شاد بادا is an optative formula 'may [it] be glad' (بادا = archaic 'may'); سپهر 'heavens/sky' is the subject placed at the end for emphasis. Literally: 'that, because of your fortune, may the heavens be glad.'
segments.s541-g69: همان مادرش نیز 'that same mother, too' is a topical phrase (همان 'that same' + مادرش 'his mother' + نیز 'also'). با او means 'with him' (accompanying), and به جای here carries the sense 'in place of / in his stead' (به جای often marks substitution). Together they set up the subject/agent: 'that mother, with him and in his stead...'.
segments.s541-g70: جهانجوی فرزند را is the object phrase 'the world-seeking / ambitious child' with را marking it as definite/focussed. رهنمای here functions as the verb 'guided' (an older/formal verbal form used in poetry) agreeing with the preceding subject (the mother). So the whole segment means 'guided the ambitious child.'
segments.s541-g71: بدو is a dative 'at/to him' indicating the target of astonishment; ماند is the past 'remained' (here 'remained in a state'); شاه جهان 'the king of the world' is the subject placed after the verb (poetic V–S order); درشگفت is an adverbial/adjunct 'in astonishment' modifying the king's state. Together: 'the king of the world remained astonished at him.'
segments.s541-g72: ازان کودک 'from that child' names the source; اندیشه‌ها is the plural object 'thoughts / counsels / ideas'; برگرفت is a past-tense verb 'took up / adopted / took away'—here meaning 'took up/adopted (his) thoughts' or 'took counsel from him.' The clause thus says the king took the child's ideas/advice.
segments.s541-g25: This chunk strings together subject and local/directional information. پسر is the head noun ('son'); بایدی follows it as an adjectival/lexical modifier here (classical/poetic use implying 'worthy/needed' or 'one that must be'); پیشم اکنون is an adverbial phrase 'before me, now' (پیشم = in my presence / before me, اکنون = now); به پای is a prepositional phrase 'to the feet of' with به + پای (پای = 'foot/base of'). Word order is noun + modifier + adverbials + prepositional phrase — a compressed classical expression expecting an implied verb like 'bring' or 'place'.
segments.s541-g26: A coordinated list of names/epithets that completes the prepositional phrase 'به پای ...'. دلارای is a proper/name or term of endearment; the following و links two adjectival/epithet words (نیروده, رهنمای) that describe the person/place—'powerful/strong' and 'guide/adviser'. In classical Persian, several descriptors can be placed after the locative to qualify the addressee.
segments.s541-g27: This is a simile: پدر (father) + بی‌پسر (sonless) followed by چون ('like, as') and then the parallel phrase پسر بی‌پدر (a son without a father). The compounds بی‌پسر and بی‌پدر use the negative prefix بی- to create adjectival phrases 'without a son' / 'without a father'; word order is straightforward Noun + adjectival complement.
segments.s541-g28: Relative/subordinate clause introduced by که ('who/that') explains the previous noun: بیگانه (a stranger) is the subject, او را (him — with را marking the definite object) is the object, and نگیرد is the negative present (does not / will not take); به بر is an idiomatic locution 'to the bosom/into the arms' (به + بر). So literally: 'that a stranger will not take him to his breast.'
segments.s541-g29: پس از من ('after me') sets a temporal/possessive frame (پس = after; از + من 'from me' = after me). بدشمن is the subject 'the hostile/enemy', and رسد is a classical intransitive/predicative verb 'reaches/attains' (used here to mean 'will come into' or 'will get'); تاج و گنج are the things attained (crown and treasure). The verb is in a timeless/present poetic form implying future result.
segments.s541-g30: This clause states the speaker's consequence. The sequence 'مرا را' is split in the gloss (م + را) and marks 'to/for me' as beneficiary/affected party. خاک سود آید literally 'dust will come as profit' (خاک = dust/ashes; سود = benefit/profit; آید = comes); this expresses that only worthless dust will be the speaker's gain. The coordination 'و درد و رنج' ('and pain and suffering') adds the miseries that will befall him. آید is present-tense classical usage implying future or inevitable result.
segments.s541-g31: به دل ('to the heart') functions as a dative/indirect object meaning 'to his heart' or 'to himself' (speaking inwardly). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical simple past); بیدار modifies مرد کهن ('the wakeful/alert old man')—the adjective before the noun is typical in poetry. The entire chunk reads 'the old, wakeful man said to his heart.'
segments.s541-g32: که introduces a reported clause 'that ...'. آمد here is a perfective/past-form 'came' used idiomatically with temporal meaning 'has come' or 'has arrived'; کنون ('now') is the adverb; روزگار سخن is a genitival/compound expression 'the time (روزگار) of speech (سخن)' — classical Persian often juxtaposes the two nouns without an ezāfe. Together: 'that now the time for speech has come.'
segments.s541-g17: This is a compact nominal clause: 'زمین هفت کشور' lists the territory ('the earth, the seven countries') and is followed by the predicative clitic-verb 'تراست'. In older/poetic Persian 'تراست' = 'ترا + است' literally 'it is to you' (i.e. 'belongs to you'); word order (noun phrase then predicate) is typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s541-g18: 'جهان یکسر از داد تو گشت راست' is a verbal clause: 'جهان' (subject) + adverb 'یکسر' ('entirely'), then the agent/cause phrase 'از داد تو' ('by/through your justice'), followed by verb 'گشت' (past of گشت, 'became') and predicate adjective 'راست' ('right/just'). The preposition 'از' here marks cause/agency and the sequence 'گشت راست' = 'was/has been made right'.
segments.s541-g19: 'چنین' ('thus') modifies the verb 'داد' (gave). Word order in the line places the verb 'داد' before its object 'پاسخ' ('answer') and the indirect object 'ورا' ('to him'), with 'شهریار' ('the king') appearing after them — a poetic inversion but grammatically the king is the giver. 'ورا' is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'to him/to her'.
segments.s541-g20: This is a vocative/exclamatory address introduced by the particle 'که‌ای' (here functioning like 'O' or 'oh'), followed by the epithets 'پاک‌دل' (pure‑hearted) and the titles 'موبد' (mōbed, Zoroastrian priest) and 'رازدار' (confidant/secret‑keeper). Word order places the adjective before the noun, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s541-g21: 'زمانه' (the times/fortune) is the subject. The phrase 'به شمشیر ما' uses 'به' to mark the instrument/agent ('by our sword'), where 'ما' functions as possessive/agent. 'راست گشت' is a compound where 'گشت' (became) takes 'راست' (right) as its predicate: 'was made right' or 'became right.'
segments.s541-g22: This is a simple coordinated noun phrase followed by the verb 'گذشت' (passed). 'غم و رنج و ناخوبی' lists miseries ('sorrow and suffering and illness'), and 'اندر گذشت' means they 'went away / passed into the past.' 'اندر' is a poetic preposition meaning 'in/among' but here idiomatically combines with 'گذشت' to mean 'passed away'.
segments.s541-g23: The clause 'مرا سال بر پنجه و یک رسید' contains the object clitic 'مرا' (to/for me) here shown split in the word list as 'م' + 'را'; idiomatically 'سال بر پنجه رسید' means 'a year reached/seized (one) by the paw' — a poetic way to say a year has overtaken the speaker. 'بر پنجه' ('on the paw') is figurative, and 'رسید' is the past verb 'arrived/reached.'
segments.s541-g24: Here 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') marks cause or source ('because of/from camphor'), 'شد' is the verb 'became' or auxiliar 'was', and the coordinated noun 'مشک و گل' ('musk and flowers/roses') is described by 'ناپدید' ('vanished'). The whole means that, owing to camphor, musk and roses disappeared (were lost/vanished).
segments.s541-g33: بدو گفت is a compact reporting clause: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (دِ + دوم) placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used as narrative 'he said'). کای شاه is a vocative address: کای = 'O' and شاه = 'king'. کهتر نواز is a two-word epithet: کهتر (literally 'lower/inferior') qualifies the addressee and نواز is an agentive/adjectival form meaning 'kind/tender' — together 'kind to the lowly' or 'gracious toward the lesser'.
segments.s541-g34: This is a string of epithets describing the addressee: جوانمرد is a noun/adjective 'chivalrous man' (an honorific), روشن‌دل is a compound adjective 'clear‑hearted/bright‑souled', و is the coordinating conjunction 'and', and سرفراز is an adjective 'proud/victorious' functioning as another epithet attached to the vocative.
segments.s541-g35: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning 'now' or 'at this very moment'. یابم is a first‑person present/subjunctive form of 'find' ('may I find' / 'I can find' in the protasis). به جان is an oath formula 'by my life/soul', and زینهار is an interjection 'be warned/take heed' used here to emphasize the speaker's vow.
segments.s541-g36: من is the emphatic subject 'I'. این رنج is the direct object 'this suffering/trouble'. بردارم is a first‑person verb form from برداشتن used here in the sense 'I will take away / I will bear' (future/optative sense in poetic narrative). از introduces the source/beneficiary relation 'from' or 'off of', and شهریار names the source/owner 'the king' — together 'I will remove this suffering from the king' (i.e. relieve him).
segments.s541-g37: بدو گفت again 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'). شاه‌ای is a vocative form 'O king' (the -ای is vocative), followed by خردمند مرد, an appositive epithet 'wise man' (خردمند = 'wise', مرد = 'man' used as a respectful address).
segments.s541-g38: چرا = 'why'. بیم جان ترا is a noun phrase 'fear for your life' where جان takes the 2nd‑person enclitic ت (rendered here as ت) meaning 'your', and را is the object marker attaching to that phrase. رنجه کرد is a light‑verb construction: رنجه (to be distressed / distress) plus کرد 'did/made' — together 'caused (you) grief' or 'troubled you'.
segments.s541-g39: بگوی is an imperative/hortative form 'say/tell' (here an exhortation). آنچ دانی = 'that which you know' (آنچ = 'that which', دانی = 'you know'). و بفزای نیز joins a further command: بفزای is an imperative 'add/augment', and نیز = 'also/too' — 'and add also'.
segments.s541-g40: ز گفت = 'from the words' or 'of the saying' (ز is the poetic form of از = 'from'). خردمند برتر is a noun phrase 'the superior/greater wise man' (خردمند = 'wise', برتر = 'higher/superior'). چه چیز is the interrogative/indefinite 'what thing' functioning as the content to be added: 'add also what the superior wise man said.'
segments.s541-g41: چنین (fronted adverb) emphasises the manner: 'thus/so'. داد is the past tense of دادن 'to give' used here as a speech verb 'gave/uttered' (past simple). پاسخ is a direct object 'an answer/reply'. بدو is the clitic+preposition 'to him' (بِ + او contracted), marking the recipient. کدخدای is the noun 'village headman' placed at the end as apposition/agent.
segments.s541-g42: که here introduces reported speech ('that' / 'saying'), followed immediately by ای as the vocative particle, so the sequence functions as an address: 'O king...'. شاه is the addressee 'king'. روشن‌دل and پاک‌رای are post‑nominal adjectives (epithets) joined by و 'and' describing the king.
segments.s541-g43: یکی here means 'a certain' (introducing an indefinite item). حقه is 'a trick' and بد qualifies it 'bad/evil'. نزد governs the following noun as 'in the presence of / at' (نزدِ + noun). گنجور شاه = 'the king's treasury' (گنجور as 'treasury' modified by شاه).
segments.s541-g44: سزدگر is a conditional/adverbial formation meaning 'if it is fitting' (سزد + گر). بخواهد is third‑person present/subjunctive of خواستن 'to want/ask' here with the sense 'let him desire/ask' or 'he should ask'. کنون 'now' and پیش گاه form a unit: پیش 'in the presence of' + گاه ('place/occasion') => 'before the (royal) presence / court'.
segments.s541-g45: به گنجور گفت = 'he said to the treasurer': به marks the indirect object 'to', گنجور is 'treasurer/treasury' (here recipient), and گفت is the past tense 'said' (the provided lemma senses list does not include the verbal sense, so the correct sense is not in the list). آنک introduces the reported content 'that/for', and او 'he' is the subject of the reported clause. زینهار is an interjection/admonition 'be warned / beware' used inside the reported speech.
segments.s541-g46: ترا is the 2nd‑person object formed from the clitic ت (your/to you) plus the object marker را — 'to you'. داد is the past verb 'gave'. آمد is the past 'came'. کنون 'now' locates the action in time. خواستار is a noun/adjective 'one who asks' or 'petitioner' describing the one who has come; here it is a predicate/nominative within the reported clause.
segments.s541-g47: بدو 'to him' (بِ + او contracted) is the indirect object. بازده is an imperative 'return it / give it back' (verb stem + imperative sense). تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that', and ببینم is the first‑person singular subjunctive/present 'I may see' or 'I see'. که چی ست is a fused clause 'what it is' (که introduces the clause; چی 'what'; ست the copula 'is').
segments.s541-g48: مگرمان is مَگر with the first‑person plural enclitic ـمان, here functioning as a rhetorical interrogative 'shouldn't we...?'. نباید is the impersonal modal 'must not / should not' (negated obligation), followed by به اندیشه 'in/with thought' (preposition + noun). زیست is the verb 'to live' (here used to express the manner of living: 'live by thought' or 'live thoughtfully').
segments.s541-g9: چنان بد که is a result clause: چنان (so/thus) + بد (bad/poorly) + که (that) introduces the result. روزی (one day) is an adverbial time word, and بیامد is the simple past (3sg) 'came' with the subject وزیر (the vizier) following the verb — verb-before-subject order is common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s541-g10: بدید is the simple past 'he saw' (here the vizier is the implied subject). آب در چهره (lit. 'water in (his) face') is a locative phrase meaning tears on the face; در introduces the location and اردشیر is the possessor/locative complement.
segments.s541-g11: بدو گفت is a common sequence meaning 'he said to him': بدو is a dative/enclitic-like prepositional phrase 'to him', and گفت is the past 'said' (classical usage placing the reporting verb before the quotation). شاها is a vocative 'O king' inserted before the quoted address; انوشه بدی here begins the reported speech (انوشه is a proper/name element and بدی follows as a noun/epithet).
segments.s541-g12: This is the content of the minister's address. روان را uses را as the definite direct-object marker 'the soul'; به اندیشه توشه is a prepositional phrase 'as a provision in (or by) thought' (به marks the role/means), and بدی (a noun 'evil/harm') functions as the provision attributed to the soul. The clause is elliptical in classical poetry (the verb 'you gave' is implied by the preceding گفت).
segments.s541-g13: ز گیتی ('from the world') is a poetic prepositional phrase (ز = از). همه کام دل is a noun phrase 'all the desires of the heart' (modifier-before-head order is common: همه 'all' + کام 'desires' + دل 'of the heart'), and یافتی is 2nd-person past 'you obtained' (verb at the end, standard Persian order).
segments.s541-g14: سر دشمن ('the enemy's head') is a genitival phrase (head as possessed by the enemy). از تخت ('from the throne') is a prepositional phrase indicating source or removal, and برتافتی is a poetic past-form (2sg) expressing triumph/overcoming — here meaning you caused the enemy to be removed from the throne or you overthrew the enemy's position.
segments.s541-g15: کنون گاه شادی is a nominal clause 'now is the time of joy' (کنون = now, گاه = time/occasion, شادی = joy). می‌خوردنست is an impersonal/classical-form compound meaning 'it is for eating' or 'one eats now' (می‌خوردن + است contracted), marking the appropriate activity for the time.
segments.s541-g16: نه negates the clause that follows. هنگام اندیشه‌ها کردنست literally 'the time for doing/thinking' — هنگام 'the time (for)', اندیشه‌ها 'thoughts', and کردنست (کردن + است) functions impersonally 'is for doing' or 'is the time to do', so the phrase means 'it is not the time for thought/deliberation.'
segments.s541-g57: نکشتم is the past tense verb with the negative prefix ن- plus the 1st-person singular ending ("I did not kill"). که here is a causal conjunction meaning "for/because," linking this clause to the following reason. فرزند بد در نهان is a noun phrase (noun + adjective + prepositional phrase): "a bad/evil offspring, hidden (in secret)" — در نهان is a prepositional phrase placed after the noun in poetic word order.
segments.s541-g58: بترسیدم is the simple past 1st-person form "I feared" (ترسیدن). از marks the object/cause of fear ("from/of"); کردگار جهان is a fixed epithet meaning "the Creator (of) the world." This clause gives the motive introduced by که in the previous segment.
segments.s541-g59: بجستم is a poetic/past verb form here meaning roughly "I yielded/obeyed" or "I removed myself" (an older verb form, irregular in modern Persian). ز is the poetic preposition "from." فرمانت (فرمان + -ت) is "your command/order" with the 2nd-person enclitic. آزرم خویش is a possessive phrase: آزرم (modesty/honor/shame) + خویش (one's own) — together "my modesty/shame." The phrase indicates the speaker put aside personal modesty because of the command.
segments.s541-g60: بریدم is a past 1st-person verb here meaning "I cut off / I severed / I broke away." هم‌اندر is a compound adverb meaning "together/at the same time/likewise," and زمان is "time/that moment" — together giving "at the same time/then." شرم خویش (شرم + خویش) is again "my shame/modesty." The verb takes this noun phrase as its object; poetically the elements are ordered for emphasis.
segments.s541-g61: بدان is an imperative/verbal form made of the prefix ب- + دان ("know") and functions as an instruction: "know this." تا introduces a purpose/result clause here meaning "so that/so no one..."; کسی is an indefinite "anyone/any person." بد نگوید مرا is a negative jussive/subjunctive construction meaning "may not say bad things of me" (نگوید is the negative/optative form of گفتن here; مرا = "me" with the object marker را attached in the written line but tokenized here).
segments.s541-g62: به is the preposition "to/into/towards." دریای تهمت is a genitive phrase "the sea of slander/accusation." نشوید is the negative-subjunctive or optative form of شدن (here "may [I/he] not become / may I not be plunged / may I not sink into"); مرا is the object pronoun "me" plus the object marker. The clause is a wish/plea not to be overwhelmed by slander.
segments.s541-g63: کنون is the temporal adverb "now." هفت‌ساله‌ست is a compound predicate meaning "is seven years old" (هفت + سال + ه + است, compressed in poetry). شاپور تو is the name with the possessive postposed تو ("your Shapur"); poetic word order places time adverb and predicate before the name.
segments.s541-g64: که introduces a dependent clause (here a benediction/wish). دایم means "always/everlasting." خرد is "wisdom." باد is the classical optative particle meaning "may it be" (used to express a wish: "may wisdom be ever..." ). دستور تو is "your command/rule/order" with the possessor تو after the noun. The clause thus reads as a wish: "May your rule be always of wisdom."

sentences.s541-s41: Send all the children out to the field; send them to play ball and polo.
sentences.s541-s42: When there was a whole plain of fair‑faced children, my heart would turn in love toward my child.
sentences.s541-s43: Let the heart bear witness to that truth; let it make me acquainted with the son.
sentences.s541-s25: He produced that treasure‑laden pouch of his and handed it over, for he had fastened it by his command.
sentences.s541-s26: The king said to him, 'What is in this pouch? Who placed this band upon the seal?'
sentences.s541-s27: He said to him, 'That warm blood is mine; cut off from the pure root, it is my shame.'
sentences.s541-s28: You entrusted to me Ardavan's daughter, so that when you demand her back, [you will find] a lifeless body.
sentences.s541-s1: When the hour of birth arrived, the secret of that affair was not laid open to the wind.
sentences.s541-s2: A son was born; afterwards Ardavan's daughter — one of kingly bearing and bright‑souled.
sentences.s541-s3: He drove the assembly away from his iwan, and gave him the name/title 'Dastur Shapur.'
sentences.s541-s4: He kept him hidden for seven years; then one became a new king, with glory and a mane.
sentences.s541-s37: Thereupon he said to the village headman, "O clear‑hearted and pure‑minded man!"
sentences.s541-s38: You have borne great suffering from this speech; may your suffering not become lasting.
sentences.s541-s39: Now bring a hundred sons of the same age as him, matching him in height, shoulder, back, and mane.
sentences.s541-s40: They should wear exactly the same garments as him together; there should be nothing more or less.
sentences.s541-s33: No child of a king remained except the moon upon the firmament.
sentences.s541-s34: I named him Shapur out of affection; may the heavens be glad because of your fortune.
sentences.s541-s35: That same mother also, alongside him and in his stead, guided the world-seeking child.
sentences.s541-s36: The king of the world stood amazed at that child, and he adopted (took up) the child's thoughts.
sentences.s541-s13: Bring the worthy son before me now, to the feet of Delārā — (one) vigorous and a guide.
sentences.s541-s14: A father without a son is like a son without a father — a son whom a stranger will not take into his arms.
sentences.s541-s15: After me, an evil enemy will attain the crown and the treasure; for me there will come dust as gain, and pain and suffering.
sentences.s541-s16: The old, wakeful man said to his heart that now the time for speech had come.
sentences.s541-s9: All the earth — the seven countries — are entirely yours; the whole world has been made right by your justice.
sentences.s541-s10: Thus the king gave him that reply: 'O pure‑hearted mōbed, keeper of secrets.'
sentences.s541-s11: The times were set right by our sword; sorrow, hardship and illness passed away.
sentences.s541-s12: A year came upon me (seized me by the paw); because of camphor, musk and flowers vanished.
sentences.s541-s17: He said to him, "O king, kind to the lowly — chivalrous, clear‑hearted and proud."
sentences.s541-s18: If I can find him now — by my life, take heed — I will relieve the monarch of this suffering.
sentences.s541-s19: He said to him, "O wise king, why did fear for your life trouble you?"
sentences.s541-s20: Tell what you know and also add what the superior wise man said.
sentences.s541-s21: Thus the village-headman answered him, "O king, clear‑hearted and pure‑minded."
sentences.s541-s22: A certain bad trick at the king's treasury — if it seems fitting, let him now petition before the royal presence.
sentences.s541-s23: He said to the treasurer, 'Beware — he has given it to you; now he has come as a petitioner.'
sentences.s541-s24: Return it to him so that I may see what it is; should we not live by careful thought?
sentences.s541-s5: It was so bad that one day the vizier came and saw water on Ardashir's face.
sentences.s541-s6: He said to him, 'O king, you have furnished the soul with evil as its provision in thought.'
sentences.s541-s7: From the world you obtained every desire of the heart; you caused the enemy's head to leave the throne.
sentences.s541-s8: Now is the time for joy and eating; it is not the time for reflection.
sentences.s541-s29: I did not kill (him), for I feared before the Creator of the world that a bad child might be hidden.
sentences.s541-s30: I obeyed your command and put aside my modesty; at the same time I cut off my shame.
sentences.s541-s31: Know this, so that no one may speak ill of me; let me not be plunged into the sea of slander.
sentences.s541-s32: Now your Shapur is seven years old; may your rule always be guided by wisdom.
